data.title: Has Mohawk Industries (MHK) Become More Attractive After Recent 24% Share Price Slide
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Mohawk Industries at around US$102.55 is a potential bargain or a value trap, you are not alone.
  • The stock has seen a 24.0% decline over the last 30 days and is down 10.3% over the past year, even though the 3 year return sits at 8.7%.
  • Recent headlines have focused on Mohawk Industries' position in the flooring and broader consumer durables market, including how it is responding to shifts in housing activity and renovation demand. This context has kept attention on the stock as investors reassess the balance between cyclicality, cost pressures and long term demand for its products.
  • Our Simply Wall St valuation checks give Mohawk Industries a 5/6 value score. This raises an obvious question: how do different valuation methods stack up for this stock, and is there an even better way to think about its value that we will come back to at the end?

Find out why Mohawk Industries's -10.3% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Mohawk Industries Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s dollars to estimate what the whole business could be worth right now.

For Mohawk Industries, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The company’s last twelve month free cash flow is about $559.9 million. Analyst inputs and Simply Wall St extrapolations point to free cash flow of $712.1 million in 2035, with intermediate years such as 2026 and 2028 projected at $601.5 million and $655.0 million respectively, all in USD and all below $1 billion.

When those projected cash flows are discounted back, the DCF model suggests an estimated intrinsic value of about $151.83 per share, compared with the recent share price of roughly $102.55. That implies the stock is trading at a 32.5% discount to this DCF estimate, indicating potential undervaluation on this model alone.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Mohawk Industries is undervalued by 32.5%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 48 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Mohawk Industries.

Approach 2: Mohawk Industries Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Mohawk Industries, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much investors are paying for each dollar of earnings. It ties directly to what the business currently earns, which is often easier to interpret than cash flow models on their own.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how quickly earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings appear. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually calls for a lower one.

Mohawk Industries currently trades on a P/E of 17.0x. That sits above the Consumer Durables industry average of about 11.4x, but below the peer group average of 19.3x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Mohawk Industries is 25.8x, which is its proprietary estimate of what the P/E could be given factors such as earnings growth, profit margin, industry, market cap and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry, because it adjusts for the company’s own fundamentals rather than assuming all firms deserve similar multiples. With the current P/E of 17.0x sitting below the 25.8x Fair Ratio, Mohawk Industries screens as undervalued on this measure.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Mohawk Industries Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to think about valuation. On Simply Wall St’s Community page you can use Narratives to connect your own story about Mohawk Industries to a set of numbers by setting assumptions for future revenue, earnings and margins. data.title: Qatar Gas Disruption Tests Norsk Hydro Earnings And Aluminum Supply Risk
data.description:
• Gas supply disruptions in Qatar have led the Qatalum aluminum plant, a major joint venture of Norsk Hydro (OB:NHY), to cut production to 60% capacity.
  • Qatalum avoided a full shutdown after QatarEnergy resumed limited gas supply under an updated agreement.
  • The event directly affects Norsk Hydro’s joint venture income and contributes to tighter global aluminum supply conditions.

Norsk Hydro is a fully integrated aluminum company, with Qatalum playing an important role in its global smelting footprint. The gas disruption in Qatar highlights how dependent aluminum production is on stable energy inputs, especially for energy intensive smelters. For you as an investor, this links an operational event at a joint venture to wider themes around energy security and supply risk in metals.

Looking ahead, the key questions are how long Qatalum operates at 60% capacity and whether further adjustments to gas supply occur. You may want to watch for any updates from OB:NHY on production volumes, contractual arrangements with QatarEnergy, and potential contingency plans for serving customers if constraints persist.

data.title: Grenke (XTRA:GLJ) Q4 EPS Surge Tests Bullish Earnings Recovery Narrative
data.description:
Grenke (XTRA:GLJ) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of €437.1 million and basic EPS of €0.77, alongside trailing twelve month revenue of €924.3 million and EPS of €1.58 that frame the year in full. Over recent periods the company has seen quarterly revenue move from €182.5 million in Q4 2024 to €437.1 million in Q4 2025, while quarterly EPS shifted from €0.34 to €0.77. See our full analysis for Grenke.

Advertisement

Margins Under Pressure At 7.5%

  • Over the last 12 months, Grenke earned €69.8 million on €924.3 million of revenue, which works out to a 7.5% net profit margin compared with 11.3% in the prior year.
  • Consensus narrative supporters point out that this lower margin sits alongside digitalisation efforts and new-market expansion, yet:
    • Trailing earnings still rose 6.6% over the year, so profitability and margin trends are telling slightly different stories.
    • Ongoing restructuring and higher risk costs are cited as headwinds, and the current 7.5% margin illustrates how those costs are flowing through to the bottom line.

Quarterly Profit Swing Across 2025

  • Within FY 2025, net income moved from a small loss of €0.7 million in Q1 to €33.9 million in Q4, while quarterly revenue ranged from €155.0 million to €437.1 million and EPS went from a slight loss to €0.77.
  • Bulls argue that cost discipline and digitalisation can support higher long term margins, and this pattern in 2025 interacts with that story in a few ways:
    • The move from a loss in Q1 to positive net income and EPS in later quarters fits with the bullish view that profitability can improve as efficiency gains build.
    • At the same time, the wide spread between early year results and Q4 highlights how sensitive earnings still are to costs and credit expenses, which bulls need to keep in mind.

Bulls say Grenke's sharp profit recovery through 2025 could be the early sign of the earnings ramp they expect. However, the step up from a Q1 loss to a strong Q4 also shows how dependent that story is on keeping costs and risk charges in check. 🐂 Grenke Bull Case

Cheap P/E, But Cash Flow Strain

  • With the share price at €13.26, Grenke trades on a P/E of 8.4x and about 34.5% below a DCF fair value of €20.24, while net profit margins are 7.5% and debt and the 3.02% dividend are not well covered by operating and free cash flows.
  • Bears focus on these weaker cash flow signals and margin compression, and the current numbers give them some support:
    • The gap between the 7.5% margin and last year’s 11.3% margin aligns with concerns that higher risk costs and expenses are eating into profitability.
    • The fact that both debt and the dividend are not well covered by cash flows shows why some investors worry that a low P/E and discount to DCF fair value might reflect real balance sheet and payout constraints.

Skeptics point out that a low 8.4x P/E and discount to DCF fair value mean less if cash flows are struggling to support debt and dividends. This is exactly the pressure they see in Grenke’s latest numbers. 🐻 Grenke Bear Case

TINC (ENXTBR:TINC) posts FY 2025 first half results

TINC (ENXTBR:TINC) has released its FY 2025 first half numbers, reporting revenue of €32.6 million and EPS of €0.50. Over the recent periods, revenue moved from €34.7 million and EPS of €0.50 in FY 2024 H1 to €37.1 million and EPS of €0.67 in FY 2024 H2, before landing at the current €32.6 million and EPS of €0.50. With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings print lines up with the widely shared narratives about TINC, and where the data challenges or supports those stories.

62.5% net margin keeps profitability high

  • Over the last 12 months, TINC reported net income of €40.6 million on revenue of €65.0 million, which works out to a net margin of 62.5% compared with 59.2% a year earlier.
  • What stands out for a bullish view is that this margin strength sits alongside FY 2025 H1 net income of €18.4 million on €32.6 million of revenue, and trailing 12 month EPS of €0.95 to €1.17. This supports the idea of a high margin, asset backed model even as individual half year numbers move around.
    • Supporters who focus on infrastructure and real asset exposure as relatively steady can point to margins above 60% on trailing revenue between €64.9 million and €71.8 million as evidence that profitability is not just a one off spike.
    • At the same time, the dip from €24.5 million net income in FY 2024 H2 to €18.4 million in FY 2025 H1 shows that even with strong margins, reported profit can shift between periods, so the bullish case has to lean on the longer trailing record rather than a single half.

EPS trend and one off loss add complexity

  • EPS for the individual halves sits at €0.50 in FY 2024 H1, €0.67 in FY 2024 H2 and €0.50 in FY 2025 H1, while trailing 12 month EPS across the last four snapshots ranges from €0.95 to €1.17. Those trailing figures also include a one off loss of €13.6 million in the last 12 months to 31 Dec 2025.
  • Critics with a bearish tilt highlight that earnings were negative over the most recent 12 month window and were hit by the €13.6 million one off loss, which makes them question how reliable the trailing EPS band of €0.95 to €1.17 really is as a guide.
    • They can point to the step down in net income from €24.5 million in FY 2024 H2 to €18.4 million in FY 2025 H1, and to the swing from positive trailing EPS of €1.17 a year ago to negative reported earnings over the latest 12 months, as signs that earnings can be volatile when one off items hit.
    • On the other hand, the fact that underlying net income, excluding extra items, still totals €40.6 million on €65.0 million of revenue over the latest 12 months provides a counterpoint, suggesting that the bearish focus on the one off loss does not fully capture the underlying profitability that the income statement is showing.
A sharp one off loss changing the earnings picture while margins stay above 60% is exactly the kind of twist that long term bulls and short term bears will argue over, so it can be useful to see how each side frames TINC's story before you decide what matters most to you. 🐻 TINC Bear Case

Share price, P/E of 13.4x and DCF fair value

  • With the share price at €11.24, the stock trades on a P/E of 13.4x versus peer and European Capital Markets industry averages of 22.1x and 15.4x, and sits about 2.6% below a DCF fair value of €11.54.

Approach 1: Ameresco Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those cash flows back to today using a required rate of return.

For Ameresco, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $592.7 million, so the valuation leans heavily on future projections. Analyst based estimates feed into the early years, with Simply Wall St extrapolating further out. By 2030, free cash flow is projected at $73 million, with annual forecasts from 2026 to 2035 all below $100 million and expressed in today’s terms through discounting.

Putting all those discounted cash flows together gives an estimated intrinsic value of $10.26 per share. Compared with the current share price, the model implies the stock is 141.0% overvalued on this methodology.

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a useful yardstick because it links what you are paying directly to the earnings the business is generating today. Investors usually accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger growth or see lower risk, and prefer a lower P/E when growth expectations are more modest or risks are higher.

Ameresco is currently trading on a P/E of 29.58x. That sits below the Construction industry average of 33.24x and above the peer group average of 18.58x, so the market is assigning it a middle of the pack valuation compared with both broader industry and closer peers.
